cataloging_subject.label: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection encoded from William Henry Black's 1842 Percy Society volume: the 124-stanza Middle English penitential-psalm paraphrase on printed pp. 1-47, the blank p. 48, Latin Appendix I on pp. 49-51, and Middle English Appendix II on pp. 51-54. The selection excludes the volume's preface, contents, and printed notes on pp. 55-70. It is neither one manuscript nor one securely unified medieval work.

catalog_summary.title: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous medieval authors; the main paraphrase is conventionally attributed to Thomas Brampton
catalog_summary.date: Early fifteenth century for the main paraphrase; later annotations give 1413 and 1414; edition published 1842
catalog_summary.region: England; no single more precise composition place is established for all three parts

titles.value: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard

Hic incipiunt septem Psalmi penitenciales, de Latino translati in Anglicum

agents.name: Anonymous medieval authors
notes: A cataloging collective for the unnamed authors or translators responsible for the three selected medieval textual units; it does not assert common authorship.

agents.name: Thomas Brampton
notes: Franciscan friar to whom the main paraphrase is conventionally attributed. Only two of six known witnesses name him, both in later additions; the identity and authorship are not independently established.

agents.name: William Henry Black
notes: 1808-1872; editor, transcriber, and collator of the 1842 Percy Society volume.

scope.description: The three medieval textual units collectively, without implying a single author

assertion.notes: No author is securely established for the complete edited selection.
Black accepted the attribution, and modern scholarship retains it conventionally, but the naming evidence occurs only in later annotations in Sloane MS 1853 and Trinity R.3.20.

scope.description: The manuscript-derived text on printed pp. 1-54

Editorial headings, source notes, and footnotes retained on printed pp. 1-54

form_statements.description: The selection combines two verse-centered Middle English parts with a Latin prose legend, quoted psalm verses, and prayers.
A 124-stanza poem in eight-line stanzas, with Latin prose epigraphs.
A Latin prose legend framing eight quoted psalm verses and two prose prayers.
A Middle English prose legend followed by eight eight-line stanzas with Latin epigraphs.

scope.description: Two prayers following the eight psalm verses

subject_statements.label: Legend of Saint Bernard and the Devil

summaries.value: An edition-level selection centered on a 124-stanza Middle English paraphrase and meditation on the Seven Penitential Psalms, conventionally but uncertainly attributed to Thomas Brampton. Black's two appendices add Latin and Middle English versions of a Saint Bernard legend concerning eight protective psalm verses, with prayers and an eight-stanza English rendering.

The main poem begins with six penitential introductory stanzas and then paraphrases the seven psalms in stanzas 7-124. Appendix I gives a Latin legend, eight Latin psalm verses, and two prayers from Harley MS 1845. Appendix II gives an English legend and eight eight-line Middle English stanzas from Royal MS 17 A XXVII.

CME00017 encodes printed pp. 1-54 of the 1842 volume, including the intentionally blank p. 48, but not the preface, contents, or Black's notes on pp. 55-70. Those omitted notes contain the fuller apparatus for Harley variants, supplied text, and editorial intervention.

The selection is cataloged at edition level because its main paraphrase and two appendices have different immediate manuscript sources and are not securely works by one author. The manuscript witnesses, Black's editorial layer, U-M encoding, and repository artifact remain distinct in lineage.

extent.description: Three top-level units: the main 124-stanza poem (992 verse lines), Latin prose Appendix I, and mixed prose-and-verse Appendix II with eight stanzas (64 verse lines). The XML has 132 LG elements, 1,056 L elements, 14 P elements, 54 PB elements, 137 HEAD elements, and seven NOTE elements. Paragraph counts are encoding structure, not a defensible item inventory.

content_parts.label: Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms
content_parts.summary: A 124-stanza Middle English penitential and devotional poem: six introductory stanzas followed by translation, paraphrase, and commentary on the seven penitential psalms, generally keyed to Latin Vulgate verses and repeatedly using the antiphon 'Ne reminiscaris, Domine'.
notes: The first six stanzas form the introduction; stanza 7 begins the first psalm.
The work is the A textual version in modern scholarship, represented by Black's Sloane-based edition.

content_parts.label: Appendix I: Latin Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard
content_parts.summary: A Latin account of Saint Bernard compelling the Devil to reveal eight efficacious psalm verses, followed by the verses and two prayers.

content_parts.label: Appendix II: Middle English Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard
content_parts.summary: A Middle English prose account of the Saint Bernard legend followed by eight Middle English eight-line devotional stanzas, each introduced by a Latin psalm-verse incipit.
notes: The XML headnote says Royal MS 17 A XXVIII. The 1842 scan reads XXVII, and current British Library descriptions show that Royal MS 17 A XXVII contains the relevant fifteenth-century material while Royal MS 17 A XXVIII is an incompatible late-sixteenth-century album.
Black explicitly regarded common authorship with the main paraphrase as uncertain.

related_works.title: Seven Penitential Psalms in the Latin Vulgate tradition
notes: The main poem translates, paraphrases, and comments upon the psalms rather than functioning as a mechanically literal translation.

evidence.citation: CME00017 XML source in the pinned CME submodule.
evidence.summary: The XML identifies the item and encodes the 124-stanza main poem, two appendices, printed page breaks, Latin quotations and prayers, Middle English verse, editorial notes, and the erroneous Royal MS 17 A XXVIII headnote.

evidence.citation: Deterministic structural inspection of CME00017.xml and alignment against Black's 1842 edition.
evidence.locator: BODY element counts and PB/@REF sequence
evidence.summary: The XML has three DIV1 units, 10 DIV2 subdivisions, 132 LG elements, 1,056 L elements, 14 P elements, 54 PB elements, 137 HEAD elements, and seven NOTE elements. Printed pp. 1-54 are represented, including an empty p. 48 before Appendix I.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00017.
evidence.summary: The item page identifies Black, Brampton, the Percy Society source edition, item title, digital text, and public-domain/No Copyright-United States status.

evidence.citation: Thomas Brampton, attributed, A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard, ed. William Henry Black (London: Percy Society, 1842).
evidence.locator: title page; preface, pp. vii-xiv; text, pp. 1-54; notes, pp. 55-70
evidence.summary: The complete scan confirms publication data, title, responsibilities, work boundary, three-part contents, source shelfmarks and folios, and the editorial apparatus omitted from CME00017.

evidence.citation: William Henry Black, preface and notes to A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms (1842).
evidence.summary: Black identifies Sloane MS 1853 as the base witness, describes and accepts its later Brampton/1414 annotation, records collation against Harley MS 1704, explains transcription conventions and emendation, identifies both appendix sources, and says common authorship of Appendix II is uncertain.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library, catalog record 59178, A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms.
evidence.summary: The record confirms the title, attributed author, editor, London Percy Society publication, T. Richards as printer, 1842 date, xiv + one leaf + 70-page extent, and series placement.

evidence.citation: Annie Sutherland, 'Performing the Penitential Psalms in the Middle Ages,' in Aspects of the Performative in Medieval Culture, ed. Manuele Gragnolati and Almut Suerbaum (Berlin and New York: de Gruyter, 2010), 15-37.
evidence.summary: Sutherland describes the early-fifteenth-century, 124-stanza paraphrase as devotional translation and commentary, explains its performative penitential and liturgical context, treats Brampton as a conventional attribution, and distinguishes A and B textual versions across six witnesses.

evidence.citation: British Library, Sloane MS 1853, Archives and Manuscripts catalog record 040-002114208.
evidence.summary: The current record identifies the parchment manuscript, extent, sixteenth-century catalog dating, and the psalm paraphrase on ff. 3-30; its dating differs from Black's early-fifteenth-century estimate for the codex.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, MS R.3.20 (James 600), online manuscript catalog.
evidence.summary: The catalog describes a fifteenth-century paper manuscript and the complete paraphrase on pp. 197-232, including a later attribution to Thomas Brampton dated 1413; the complete 406-canvas IIIF object is publicly available under CC BY-NC 4.0.

evidence.citation: British Library, Harley MS 1845, Archives and Manuscripts catalog record 040-002047676.
evidence.summary: The record identifies an imperfect Sarum Book of Hours on parchment, produced in southern England or London in the second or third quarter of the fifteenth century; Black locates Appendix I on ff. 15r-16v.

evidence.citation: British Library, Royal MS 17 A XXVII and its fifteenth-century component, records 040-002107258 and 041-003475038.
evidence.summary: The records identify the codex and its English and Latin prayer material on ff. 71r-97v from the first quarter of the fifteenth century, supporting Black's Appendix II source at ff. 86v-88v.

evidence.citation: British Library, Royal MS 17 A XXVIII, Archives and Manuscripts catalog record 040-002107259.
evidence.summary: The current record describes a 25-folio late-sixteenth-century calligraphic album, demonstrating that the XML headnote's XXVIII shelfmark cannot be the fifteenth-century Appendix II source shown as XXVII in the 1842 scan.
The linked lineage record supplies stable entity IDs for the source edition, digital encoding, repository artifact, manuscript witnesses, and facsimiles used by metadata locators and bindings.

open_questions.question: Who composed the main paraphrase, and what weight should be given to the later Brampton attributions dated 1413 and 1414?
next_steps: Inspect the attribution hands in complete images of Sloane MS 1853 and Trinity R.3.20.
Review post-Sutherland scholarship on Brampton's identity, the six-witness stemma, and the relative dates of the A and B versions.
Keep author, annotator, and composition-date claims separate unless stronger evidence emerges.

open_questions.question: How should the physical date and textual layers of Sloane MS 1853 be reconciled across Black's early-fifteenth-century description, later scholarship's fifteenth-century dating, and the current British Library catalog's sixteenth-century date?
next_steps: Obtain complete color images and current curatorial assessment of support, script, decoration, and added attribution hand.
Consult modern codicological and palaeographical scholarship before assigning a single witness date.

open_questions.question: Is the Middle English Legendary Psalter in Appendix II related authorially or textually to the main penitential-psalm paraphrase beyond Black's juxtaposition?
next_steps: Compare dialect, meter, vocabulary, and transmission across Royal MS 17 A XXVII and witnesses of the main poem.
Review scholarship on the Saint Bernard eight-verses legend and its Middle English versions.

notes: Work metadata describes the U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection; witness holdings, facsimile completeness, access, and image rights remain in the linked lineage manifest.
The traditional Thomas Brampton attribution is scoped only to the main paraphrase and marked disputed.

Royal MS 17 A XXVII is used as Appendix II's source identity because the 1842 scan and current catalog evidence agree; the XML's XXVIII reading is documented as an encoding error, not silently propagated.
The absence of Black's pp. 55-70 notes matters: the encoding retains selected editorial interventions but not the complete printed apparatus that explains them.
